title: Can one pay Zakat on behalf of someone else?
tags: zakat on behalf of someone else
can someone else give your zakat
stanceSummary: Zakat is an obligation on the **owner** of the wealth. A husband may voluntarily gift his wife money to pay her Zakat — it becomes her property and she pays it — but he is **not obligated** to do so, nor can his wife demand it. Similarly, a guardian of an orphan is not obligated to pay the orphan's Zakat from their own pocket; they may and should take 2.5% from the orphan's wealth itself. Voluntary payment on behalf of another is permissible and praiseworthy, but it cannot be demanded.
alternateQuestions: Can a husband pay Zakat for his wife?
Who is responsible for paying Zakat on a wife's gold?
Can a guardian pay Zakat from an orphan's wealth?
Is it obligatory for a husband to pay his wife's Zakat?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2021 Ask Shaykh YQ video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs) (starting at [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=0s)), Yasir Qadhi addresses whether Zakat may be paid on behalf of another person.

- Zakat is **an obligation on the owner of the wealth** — if the owner is an adult of sound mind, they are primarily responsible
- A person **may voluntarily** pay Zakat on behalf of someone else — it functions as a gift: the paying party gifts the amount to the owner, who then uses it to fulfill their own Zakat obligation
- This **cannot be demanded** — a wife cannot demand her husband pay her Zakat, nor can an heir demand it of anyone else
- **Guardian of an orphan**: the guardian should pay Zakat from the orphan's own wealth (2.5%), not from their personal funds — this is sufficient and correct; there is no obligation on the guardian to pay from their own pocket
- Whether a wife's gold jewelry is even subject to Zakat is a **highly contested** question among the four schools — Yasir Qadhi notes he did not resolve this in the clip and would address it separately

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question — [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=0s)

Brother Abdur-Rahman emails asking: upon whom is Zakat due — the owner of the wealth, or the person responsible for the owner? Specifically, he asks whether a woman who has gold but does not work may have her husband pay the Zakat on her wealth, or whether she must pay it from her own wealth.

### Zakat Is an Obligation on the Owner — [0:30](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=30s)

Yasir Qadhi explains: **Zakat is an obligation due from the wealth to the poor.** If the owner of the wealth is an adult of sound mind, then that owner is primarily responsible for paying it.

### What About Incapacitated Owners? — [1:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=60s)

If the owner is not an adult of sound mind — for example, an orphaned child whose deceased father left them $100,000 — then the **guardian** of that child shall take the Zakat from the child's wealth (2.5%) and distribute it. The Zakat in this case comes from the orphan's property, not from the guardian's personal funds.

### Voluntary Payment Is Permissible — [2:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=120s)

However, this obligation *can* be transferred voluntarily. For example, if a husband wishes to pay his wife's Zakat:
1. He **gifts** her the amount of her Zakat — say, $1,000
2. That money becomes **her property**
3. She then pays that $1,000 as her Zakat
4. Her obligation is fulfilled

This is like any other gift — permissible and praiseworthy. Yasir Qadhi even says we *expect* a generous husband to do this if his wife has non-liquid assets (like gold jewelry) and no cash to sell them. But the Sharia **does not require** it of the husband.

### Parenthetical: Zakat on Gold Jewelry — [3:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=180s)

Yasir Qadhi pauses to note that Zakat on a woman's personal gold jewelry is itself one of the most hotly debated topics in all of Zakat jurisprudence, going back to the time of the Sahaba. The four schools differ significantly on whether personal-use gold jewelry is even subject to Zakat. He does not resolve this question in this clip and promises a separate detailed discussion.

### The Guardian of an Orphan — [5:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=300s)

If an orphan has wealth and the guardian (uncle, cousin, etc.) wishes to pay the Zakat from their own funds out of generosity — no problem, Allah will bless them for it. However, it is **not obligatory** for the guardian to do this. The correct and sufficient approach is to take 2.5% from the orphan's wealth and distribute it as Zakat. Allah knows how much is being taken.

### Summary — [7:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DyBFTfBoBOs&t=420s)

A person **may** volunteer to pay Zakat on behalf of another, in which case it functions as a gift. It **cannot be demanded** by the wealth's owner from another person. And Allah knows best.
